Title: Complex Task Planning for General-Purpose Service Robots
Affiliations: Advanced Intelligence & Robotics Research Center, Tamagawa University, 6-1-1 Tamagawagakuen, Tokyo, Machida, 194-8610, Japan; Signal Processing Department, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Circuito Exterior S/N, Ciudad Universitaria, Coyoacán, Mexico City, 04510, Mexico; Visual and Multimodal Applied Learning Laboratory, Polytechnic University of Turin, Torino, 10129, Italy

Abstract: Service robots, in contrast to industrial robots, are devices aimed to operate in the service sector in replacement of or collaboration with human performers— in particular, domestic service robots carry out daily household chores. Recently, their popularity has increased and the range and difficulty of the activities they can perform are reaching amazing performances. There have been proposed several systems that can generalize single tasks such as object manipulation or scene understanding but they still fail on complex task planning, i.e., when and where they can perform such tasks. In this work, we propose the use of expert systems as a core module to understand user commands, infer the task context, request missing information, and plan action where each step of the plan may consist of basic state machines to generalized deep-learning models. © Fuji Technology Press Ltd.

Title: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Source title: Proceedings - 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation, CONISOFT 2024
Affiliations: Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Iimas, Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Mexico City, Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Facultad de Ingenería, Dpto. de Computación, Mexico City, Mexico
Authors with affiliations: Lopez-Villafan R., Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Iimas, Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Mexico City, Mexico; Barcenas E., Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Facultad de Ingenería, Dpto. de Computación, Mexico City, Mexico
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic K with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as 'there are at least n+1 individuals satisfying a certain property', where n is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: 'if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task', or epistemic logic:'if more than 2 people know P, then P is known'. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.  © 2024 IEEE.
Author Keywords: automated reasoning; graded modalities; hypersequents; proof theory

Title: Transforming Software Development: A Study on the Integration of Multi-Agent Systems and Large Language Models for Automatic Code Generation

Affiliations: Universidad veracruzana, Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Xalapa, Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Facultad de Ingeniería, Ciudad de México, Mexico

Abstract: This paper explores the integration of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) and Large Language Models (LLMs) for auto-matic code generation, addressing the limitations of traditional manual coding. By conducting a comprehensive review of existing literature and analyzing a practical case study, we demonstrate how MAS and LLMs can collaboratively enhance software development processes. The research focuses on the technical and theoretical challenges of this integration, highlighting the potential for improved productivity, adaptability, and quality in code generation. The findings contribute to AI-based software engineering by revealing new research directions in collective intelligence and automated programming.  © 2024 IEEE.

Title: Expert system for the parabolic trough collector control through classical and conformable transfer functions in ANNi-PSO

Universidad 1001, Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos, 62209, Mexico
Abstract: This research presents an innovative approach to controlling parabolic trough collectors (PTC) using a multivariate inverse artificial neural network (ANNi) and classical and conformal transfer functions. The objective is to maximize the thermal energy generation of the PTC and maintain the constant output temperature, even in variable weather conditions. Three controllers were designed: the first optimizes the input flow, the second adjusts both the input flow and temperature simultaneously and the third prioritizes the flow and adjusts the temperature when necessary. Results reveal that controllers with Swish and CSwish transfer functions achieve superior performance by providing optimal flow and temperature, with increases of up to 8.5% and 5.2% in water flow and savings of up to 1.25% in temperature. Although the first controller faces challenges in staying on the baseline due to adverse weather conditions, the excellent performance of the CSwish controller stands out, outperforming the others by 2.26%. On the other hand, the second controller demonstrates excellent tracking of the reference temperature, with an average steady-state error of ±0.2 ℃. In contrast, the third controller has an average tracking error of only 1 °C. These findings highlight the importance of expert systems in efficiently using renewable energy and underline the crucial role of transfer functions in the design of model-based controllers. © 2025 Elsevier Ltd

Title: Towards a Collaborative Game Designer Using the CCDSF Framework
Affiliations: Posgrado en Ciencia e Ingeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, 04510, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, 04510, Mexico
Authors with affiliations: Pérez-Romero J., Posgrado en Ciencia e Ingeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, 04510, Mexico; Aguilar W., Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, 04510, Mexico
Abstract: The Computational Creative Design Systems Framework (CCDSF) was formulated from a mixed perspective of the fields of design research and computational creativity to describe, analyze, and model Computational Creative Design Systems (CCDSs) either as autonomous agents or as collaborative systems involving two or more design agents in purely computational or human-computer interaction mode. In this paper, we present the first proof-of-concept (PoC) of the CCDSF, demonstrating the incremental construction of a human-computer collaborative CCDS for the design of puzzle-type video games. This CCDS is composed of a computational design agent that evolves through three versions—assistant, apprentice, and creative assistant—each acquiring new technical and creative capabilities and assuming roles with higher levels of collaboration. The system includes a graphical user interface (collaborative environment) that has been conceptualized, implemented, and evaluated following the principles of user interface, user experience, and interaction design. Each building block of the CCDSF was systematically designed, encompassing the top-level experience-dynamics-mechanics design schema down to the underlying perception-action strategies. While further research is needed, our collaborative CCDS, presented as a PoC of the CCDSF, demonstrates its potential to aid human designers in generating viable designs and enhancing their technical and creative performance in various ways. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to The Japanese Society for Artificial Intelligence and Springer Nature Japan KK, part of Springer Nature 2024.

Title: Keep it Simple: Understanding Natural Language Commands for General-Purpose Service Robots
Source title: 2024 IEEE/SICE International Symposium on System Integration, SII 2024
Affiliations: School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Biorobotics Laboratory, Mexico; Tamagawa University, Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Japan
Authors with affiliations: Ortuno-Chanelo S., School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Biorobotics Laboratory, Mexico; Contreras L., Tamagawa University, Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Japan; Savage J., School of Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Biorobotics Laboratory, Mexico; Okada H., Tamagawa University, Advance Intelligence and Robotics Research Center, Japan
Abstract: Service robots are designed to perform useful tasks for humans, which involve managing and combining a variety of skills in the form of global and local plans to solve any given task. In this work, we propose a framework to process natural language commands for general-purpose service robots where the robot should perform an arbitrary spoken command requested by a non-expert operator. Our system uses a Natural Language Processing (NLP) parser and a Conceptual Dependency (CD) builder to create CD structures that an expert system can employ to generate a global plan that the robot will execute. An extra simplification step using Large Language Models (LLM) was tested and evaluated in order to improve the accuracy of the system. Finally, our system has been tested in challenging environments in robot competitions and we have achieved promising results.  © 2024 IEEE.
Index Keywords: Intelligent robots; Mobile robots; Natural language processing systems; Dependency structures; Language model; Language processing; Natural languages; Robot competitions; Service robots; Simple++; System use; Expert systems

Title: On the attitude consensus of rigid bodies using exponential coordinates

Abstract: Consensus is an emergent behavior that occurs when the robots or agents of a robotic network reach an agreement and converge to a common state. Consensus is essential in many multi-robot coordination tasks. This paper addresses the attitude consensus problem in a network of fully actuated rigid bodies. We propose two consensus control laws based on the exponential coordinates of rotation. The first controller uses absolute attitude measurements, whereas the second one employs relative measurements. Both control algorithms avoid potential singularities of the exponential coordinates and achieve almost global asymptotic stability. Under certain conditions on the network topology, the proposed consensus protocols work for either bidirectional and unidirectional communication channels. Finally, we provide numerical simulations and experimental results to assess the performance of the proposed control laws. © 2025
